modules.title: Stock market basics
modules.sub: Shares, DSE, and how buying works

lessons.title: What is a share?
lessons.body: A share is a tiny piece of ownership in a company. If the company does well and grows, the share can become worth more; if it does badly, worth less. You make money two ways: the price rising, and dividends (a slice of profit the company pays out).
lessons.takeaway: A share = part-ownership of a real business.

lessons.title: What is the DSE?
lessons.body: The Dhaka Stock Exchange (DSE) is the marketplace where shares of Bangladeshi listed companies are bought and sold. Prices move all day during market hours (Sun–Thu, 10:00–14:30) based on supply and demand.
lessons.takeaway: The DSE is where DSE-listed shares trade.

lessons.title: You need a BO account
lessons.body: To buy shares you open a BO (Beneficiary Owner) account through a licensed broker — a one-time setup with your NID and bank details. You add money to the broker account, then place buy/sell orders in their app. Shares settle to your BO account, usually the next business day.
lessons.takeaway: Broker + BO account = your gateway to the market.

lessons.title: This app doesn't trade
lessons.body: DSE Analyst only shows analysis of public data. It never places orders and isn't connected to any broker. You buy and sell yourself, in your broker's app. Every decision is yours.
lessons.takeaway: Analysis here, orders in your broker app.

quiz.q: What does owning a share mean?
quiz.why: A share is a small ownership stake in the business.

quiz.q: When is the DSE open?
quiz.why: DSE trades Sunday to Thursday, 10:00 to 14:30.

quiz.q: Can this app place a buy order for you?
opts: No — you order in your broker app
quiz.why: The app is analysis-only; you trade through your broker.

modules.title: Reading this app
modules.sub: The score, the colours, live vs daily

lessons.title: The 0–100 score
lessons.body: Each stock gets a data score that blends fundamentals, financial health, dividend safety, and how clean vs manipulation-prone its price history looks. Higher = stronger and cleaner. It is a descriptive data score — not an investment rating, recommendation, or prediction.
lessons.takeaway: The score describes quality + cleanliness, not a buy signal.

lessons.title: Green, amber, red
lessons.body: Colours mean the same everywhere: green = stronger / good, amber = caution, red = weaker / risk. A red dot on a hot gainer is a warning before you tap. Bands: Strong 80+, Fair 60–79, Mixed 40–59, Weak 20–39, High-risk under 20.
lessons.takeaway: Green good, red risky — consistently.

lessons.title: Live price vs daily analysis
lessons.body: Prices update live during market hours. The deep analysis — score, health, cycles, setups — refreshes after each market close (plus intraday setup scans). So a price can move before the analysis catches up; that's why each screen shows an 'as of' time.
lessons.takeaway: Price is live; analysis is timestamped.

lessons.title: It explains, you decide
lessons.body: The app shows measured evidence — what worked, what failed, the risks, the levels — but never tells you to buy or sell. Read the evidence, then make your own call.
lessons.takeaway: Evidence in, decision yours.

quiz.q: A score of 85 means…
quiz.why: The score describes quality and cleanliness — not a prediction or a buy call.

quiz.q: Red usually means…
quiz.why: Red flags weakness or risk across the app.

quiz.q: Why show an 'as of' time?
opts: Price is live but analysis refreshes on a schedule
quiz.why: Live price can move ahead of the scheduled analysis refresh.

lessons.title: Candles
lessons.body: Each candle shows a day's open, high, low and close. A tall green body = buyers won the day; a tall red body = sellers did. Long wicks show rejection — price went there but came back. Patterns are tendencies, not certainties.
lessons.takeaway: A candle = one day's battle between buyers and sellers.

lessons.title: Moving average & value zone
lessons.body: A moving average (e.g. 50-day) smooths price into a trend line. The 'value zone' is where the stock has usually traded around its average. Price inside or below the zone = its normal/lower range; far above = stretched.
lessons.takeaway: Value zone = the stock's usual range vs its average.

lessons.title: RSI — overbought / oversold
lessons.body: RSI runs 0–100. Above ~70 = overbought (run hot, often pauses); below ~30 = oversold (beaten down, sometimes bounces). On the DSE, deeply oversold sound stocks have historically bounced more reliably than chasing overbought ones.
lessons.takeaway: High RSI = hot; low RSI = beaten down.

lessons.title: Support & resistance
lessons.body: Support is a price where buyers have stepped in before (a floor); resistance is where sellers have (a ceiling). The app lists these levels with the live distance. They are observed levels, not targets or guarantees.
lessons.takeaway: Support = floor, resistance = ceiling — observed, not promised.

quiz.q: A long green candle body means…
quiz.why: A tall green body means the close was well above the open — buyers won.

quiz.q: RSI of 78 suggests…
quiz.why: Above ~70 is overbought territory.

quiz.q: Support is…
opts: A level where buyers stepped in before
quiz.why: Support is an observed level of past buying — not a guarantee.

lessons.title: Pump-and-dump signs
lessons.body: A stock that spikes hard on huge volume with weak or falling earnings is a classic manipulation pattern. The prettiest charts often sit on the worst companies. The app flags these; treat them as caution, not opportunity.
lessons.takeaway: Explosive chart + weak fundamentals = danger, not a gift.

lessons.title: Z-category
lessons.body: DSE puts failing companies (no AGM, accumulated losses, insolvent) in Z-category. They are the highest-risk group, often can't be bought on margin, and can keep falling. A high price on a Z stock is not a bargain.
lessons.takeaway: Z = the riskiest shelf on the exchange.

lessons.title: Chasing overbought
lessons.body: Buying after a stock has already run far above its value zone (overbought) usually gives worse odds on the DSE than waiting for a dip. Structure going up is not the same as a good entry price.
lessons.takeaway: A rising stock can still be a bad entry.

lessons.title: Over-concentration
lessons.body: Putting a big share of your money in one stock means one bad day hurts everything. If any single holding is more than ~40% of your portfolio, that's a concentration risk the app will warn about.
lessons.takeaway: Don't let one stock decide your whole outcome.

quiz.q: A stock spikes on huge volume but earnings are falling. This is…
quiz.why: Big spike + weak fundamentals is the pump-and-dump signature.

quiz.q: Z-category means…
quiz.why: Z-category holds failing, highest-risk companies.

quiz.q: One stock is 55% of your portfolio. That is…
quiz.why: Too much in one name means one bad day moves everything.

lessons.title: Diversify
lessons.body: Spread money across several stocks and sectors so no single company can sink you. A common beginner mistake is putting everything into one 'hot tip'.
lessons.takeaway: Many small bets beat one big gamble.

lessons.title: Position sizing
lessons.body: Decide in advance how much of your money goes into each stock, and never invest money you can't afford to lose or that is borrowed. Size each position so one loss won't hurt you badly.
lessons.takeaway: Size positions so one loss can't wreck you.

lessons.title: Dividends & record dates
lessons.body: To receive a dividend you must hold the share before the record date. Companies announce dividends and record dates; the app shows the upcoming ones. A high yield on a weak company can be a trap if the payout isn't sustainable.
lessons.takeaway: Hold before the record date; check the payout is sustainable.

lessons.title: When to reconsider
lessons.body: Reasons people re-examine a holding: the fundamentals deteriorate (dividend cut, distress), the price stretches far above its value zone, or the original reason for buying is no longer true. The app surfaces these; the decision stays yours.
lessons.takeaway: Reconsider when the reason you bought changes.

quiz.q: Diversifying means…
quiz.why: Diversification spreads risk across many holdings.

quiz.q: To get a dividend you must…
quiz.why: You must hold the share before the record date.

quiz.q: A very high dividend yield on a weak company…
opts: Can be a trap if unsustainable
quiz.why: If the company can't sustain the payout, the yield can be a trap.

modules.title: Candlestick basics
modules.sub: The building block of every chart on this app

lessons.title: What it is
lessons.body: A candlestick shows one trading session's price action in a single shape: where the price opened, where it closed, and the highest/lowest points it touched. A thick body between open and close, with thin "wicks" (or "shadows") above and below marking the extremes.
lessons.takeaway: One candle = one session's whole story: open, high, low, close — in one glance.

lessons.title: How it works
lessons.body: If the close is ABOVE the open, the body is usually shown GREEN (or hollow) — price rose that session. If the close is BELOW the open, the body is RED (or filled) — price fell. The wicks show how far price wandered before settling: a long upper wick means buyers pushed price up but it got rejected back down; a long lower wick means sellers pushed it down but buyers dragged it back up.
lessons.takeaway: Body colour = up or down session. Wick length = how much it wandered before settling.

lessons.title: Worked example
lessons.body: Say a stock opens at ৳100, rises to ৳108 during the day, dips to ৳98, then closes at ৳105. The candle body runs from ৳100 (open) to ৳105 (close) — green, since it closed higher. The upper wick reaches ৳108 (the day's high) and the lower wick reaches ৳98 (the day's low). That single shape tells you: buyers won the session, but there was a real dip along the way.
lessons.takeaway: A candle's shape often matters as much as its colour — a green body with a long lower wick reads very differently from a green body with almost no wick at all.

quiz.q: A candle's body is green. What does that mean?
quiz.why: Green (or hollow) bodies mean the session's close was above its open.

quiz.q: A long lower wick on a candle usually means:
opts: Sellers pushed price down but buyers dragged it back up before close
quiz.why: A long lower wick shows price fell hard intra-session, then recovered — a rejection of the lower price.

modules.title: Reading a candle chart
modules.sub: What a whole sequence of candles is telling you

lessons.title: What it is
lessons.body: A candle chart strings many sessions' candles together in time order, so you can see not just one day's action but the FLOW — is the stock trending, ranging, or reversing.
lessons.takeaway: One candle tells you a day. A sequence of candles tells you a story.

lessons.title: How it works
lessons.body: Look at the pattern of highs and lows: rising highs AND rising lows = an uptrend. Falling highs AND falling lows = a downtrend. Highs and lows staying roughly level = a range (sideways, no clear direction). Clusters of long wicks in one direction often mark a turning point, where the market tried to keep going one way and got repeatedly rejected.
lessons.takeaway: Compare each candle's high/low to the ones before it — the direction of that comparison is the trend.

lessons.title: Worked example
lessons.body: On this app's ticker chart, if you see 10 candles where each session's low is higher than the session before, that's a clean uptrend — the stock keeps finding buyers at a rising floor. If instead you see several long-upper-wick candles bunched near a price ceiling, each failing to close near its high, that's often a stock repeatedly getting rejected at resistance.
lessons.takeaway: You don't need every candle to agree — you're looking for the OVERALL drift of highs and lows, and where wicks cluster.

quiz.q: Rising highs AND rising lows across several candles usually signals:
quiz.why: Both highs and lows stepping up together is the classic signature of an uptrend.

quiz.q: Several long-upper-wick candles clustered at the same price level often mark:
opts: A level where the price keeps getting rejected (resistance)
quiz.why: Repeated rejection at the same price level is exactly what resistance means.

modules.title: Moving averages (50 & 200-day)
modules.sub: Smoothing out the noise to see the underlying trend

lessons.title: What it is
lessons.body: A moving average (MA) is the average closing price over the last N sessions, recalculated every day. The 50-day MA reflects roughly the last 2-3 months; the 200-day MA reflects roughly the last 9-10 months. It smooths out day-to-day noise so you can see the underlying trend.
lessons.takeaway: An MA is just a rolling average price — it lags behind today's price by design, to filter out noise.

lessons.title: How it works
lessons.body: Price above its 200-day MA is generally considered in a longer-term uptrend; below it, a longer-term downtrend. The 50-day MA reacts faster and is used to judge the shorter-term trend. When the 50-day MA crosses above the 200-day MA (a "golden cross"), it's a classic — though lagging — signal that momentum has turned up; the reverse ("death cross") signals the opposite.
lessons.takeaway: Price vs. 200-MA = the long-term trend. Price vs. 50-MA = the short-term trend. Crosses are lagging, not predictive.

lessons.title: Worked example
lessons.body: Suppose a stock's price is ৳120, its 50-day MA is ৳115, and its 200-day MA is ৳100. Price is above BOTH — a stock in a longer uptrend, currently also above its shorter-term average. If price later fell to ৳98 (below both MAs), that would flag a break of both the short- and long-term trend, worth noticing even though MAs themselves are backward-looking.
lessons.takeaway: MAs describe where price HAS been trending, not where it will go next — they're a lagging read, useful for context.

quiz.q: Price sitting above its 200-day MA is generally read as:
opts: A stock about to be suspended
quiz.why: Being above the 200-day average is the standard longer-term-uptrend context read — not a guarantee of anything forward.

quiz.q: Moving averages are best described as:
opts: A lagging, smoothed view of where price has been trending
quiz.why: MAs average PAST closes — useful for trend context, but they lag price by construction.

modules.title: RSI (Relative Strength Index)
modules.sub: A 0-100 gauge of how stretched a recent move is

lessons.title: What it is
lessons.body: RSI is a 0-100 number that measures how strong recent gains have been relative to recent losses, over a lookback window (usually 14 sessions). It doesn't measure price level — it measures the SPEED and SIZE of recent moves.
lessons.takeaway: RSI answers 'how stretched is this recent move', on a 0-100 scale — not 'is the price high or low'.

lessons.title: How it works
lessons.body: Traditionally, RSI above 70 is called 'overbought' — a fast, large recent rise that has historically been more likely to pause or pull back. RSI below 30 is called 'oversold' — a fast, large recent fall, historically more likely to bounce. RSI between roughly 40-60 is unremarkable — no strong recent stretch either way.
lessons.takeaway: RSI ≥70 = stretched up (historically pause/pullback zone). RSI ≤30 = stretched down (historically bounce zone).

lessons.title: Worked example
lessons.body: This app's own backtests found that a 3-signal oversold "washout" — RSI in the low 30s, price well below its Bollinger lower band, and a large stretch below the 50-day MA all agreeing at once — historically bounced roughly 62-65% of the time, +6-7% over the next 10 sessions on artificial-activity-flagged names. RSI alone is a weaker signal; it earns its value stacked with other confirming reads, which is exactly how the Outlook meter on the ticker page uses it.
lessons.takeaway: RSI is most useful combined with other signals (support levels, Bollinger position), not read alone.

quiz.q: An RSI reading of 25 is traditionally described as:
quiz.why: RSI below ~30 is the traditional 'oversold' zone — a fast recent fall, historically more bounce-prone.

quiz.q: RSI mainly measures:
opts: How fast/large recent gains have been vs. recent losses
quiz.why: RSI is a momentum measure of recent gains vs. losses, not a price-level or fundamentals measure.

modules.title: Bollinger Bands
modules.sub: A volatility envelope around the moving average

lessons.title: What it is
lessons.body: Bollinger Bands are three lines: a middle moving average, and an upper/lower band drawn a statistical distance (usually 2 standard deviations) above and below it. The bands widen when the stock is more volatile and narrow when it's calmer.
lessons.takeaway: Bollinger Bands are a volatility envelope — they widen and narrow with how much the stock is actually moving.

lessons.title: How it works
lessons.body: Price touching or breaking the UPPER band suggests it's stretched to the upside relative to its own recent volatility. Price touching or breaking the LOWER band suggests it's stretched to the downside. A period where the bands squeeze very narrow often precedes a bigger move (in either direction) once volatility picks back up.
lessons.takeaway: Touching the upper/lower band = stretched relative to recent volatility, not an automatic buy/sell trigger.

lessons.title: Worked example
lessons.body: If a stock's price sits below its lower Bollinger band while RSI is also under 30, that's two independently-built stretch measures agreeing — exactly the kind of confirming stack this app's washout signal looks for. On its own, a single lower-band touch is a much weaker, noisier read.
lessons.takeaway: Bollinger Bands work best as ONE confirming read among several — not a standalone trigger.

quiz.q: Bollinger Bands widen when:
opts: The stock is calmer than usual
The stock is more volatile than usual
quiz.why: The bands are built from recent volatility — they widen exactly when the stock is swinging more.

quiz.q: Price breaking below the lower Bollinger band means:
opts: The stock is stretched to the downside relative to its own recent volatility
quiz.why: It's a stretch reading, not a guarantee — useful mainly combined with other signals.

modules.title: MACD
modules.sub: Comparing two moving averages to gauge momentum

lessons.title: What it is
lessons.body: MACD (Moving Average Convergence/Divergence) tracks the gap between a faster moving average and a slower one. When the faster average pulls away from the slower one, momentum is strengthening in that direction; when they converge, momentum is fading.
lessons.takeaway: MACD measures whether momentum (the GAP between a fast and slow average) is strengthening or fading.

lessons.title: How it works
lessons.body: MACD is usually plotted with a signal line (a moving average of MACD itself). When MACD crosses ABOVE its signal line, that's read as strengthening upward momentum; crossing BELOW is read as strengthening downward momentum. Like moving-average crosses, MACD crosses are LAGGING — they confirm a momentum shift already underway rather than predicting one in advance.
lessons.takeaway: MACD crossing its signal line flags a momentum shift that's already begun — it's a confirming, lagging read, not a leading one.

lessons.title: Worked example
lessons.body: If a stock has been falling and MACD starts rising toward, then crosses above, its signal line while price is still soft, that can mark the point where selling pressure is fading even before price itself turns — useful as ONE piece of context, best read together with support levels and RSI rather than acted on alone.
lessons.takeaway: Treat a MACD cross as one data point in a broader picture, not a standalone timing tool.

quiz.q: MACD is built from:
opts: The gap between a faster and a slower moving average
quiz.why: MACD is literally the difference between two moving averages of different speeds.

quiz.q: MACD crosses are best described as:
opts: Leading signals that predict the future
Lagging signals that confirm a momentum shift already underway
quiz.why: Like MA crosses, MACD crosses confirm what's already started, rather than predicting what's next.

modules.title: Volume
modules.sub: How much actually traded, not just where price went

lessons.title: What it is
lessons.body: Volume is simply the number of shares that changed hands in a session. Price tells you WHERE the stock moved; volume tells you how much conviction (or how little) was behind that move.
lessons.takeaway: Price is direction. Volume is how much weight was behind that direction.

lessons.title: How it works
lessons.body: A price move on HIGH volume is generally read as more meaningful/durable than the same move on thin volume — more participants agreed with it. A big price move on unusually LOW volume can be a warning sign that few real participants are behind it. Sudden volume spikes without a clear news reason are also one of the patterns this app's price-integrity checks watch for.
lessons.takeaway: A move on heavy volume carries more weight than the same-looking move on thin volume.

lessons.title: Worked example
lessons.body: Two stocks both rise 5% in a day. Stock A does it on triple its normal volume — many participants bought in, a more durable-looking move. Stock B does it on a fraction of its normal volume — far fewer participants involved, a thinner, less convincing move that's more easily reversed the next session.
lessons.takeaway: When judging a price move, always glance at whether volume confirms it or looks unusually thin.

quiz.q: A price rise on unusually HIGH volume is generally read as:
opts: Less meaningful than the same rise on low volume
More meaningful — more participants were behind it
A sign the stock will be delisted
quiz.why: Higher participation behind a move is usually read as a stronger, more durable signal.

quiz.q: Volume mainly tells you:
opts: How many shares changed hands — the conviction behind a move
quiz.why: Volume is a measure of participation/conviction, separate from the direction price itself moved.

modules.title: Support & resistance
modules.sub: The price levels where a stock has repeatedly reacted before

lessons.title: What it is
lessons.body: Support is a price level where a stock has historically stopped falling and bounced. Resistance is a level where it has historically stopped rising and reversed down. Both are built from the stock's OWN past reactions — not universal numbers.
lessons.takeaway: Support = a floor the price has bounced off before. Resistance = a ceiling it has been turned back at before.

lessons.title: How it works
lessons.body: These levels aren't exact lines — think of them as zones. The MORE times a level has been tested and held, the more participants tend to watch it, though nothing guarantees it holds the next time too. A broken support level often becomes resistance afterward (and vice versa), since the price at which some participants once bought becomes a level where they may now sell just to get back to even.
lessons.takeaway: Levels are zones, not exact lines — and one that breaks can flip roles (old support -> new resistance).

lessons.title: Worked example
lessons.body: This app's Levels card shows a stock's nearest support and resistance, computed from its own price history — e.g. "Support ৳45.20, Resistance ৳52.80" means the stock has repeatedly found buyers near ৳45 and repeatedly stalled near ৳53 in the past. Price sitting near the support end of that range is what this app calls being "in the entry zone" — historically where forward outcomes measured best; price stretched well above resistance is the opposite.
lessons.takeaway: The Levels card on every ticker page is exactly this — support/resistance measured from that stock's own history.

quiz.q: Support is best described as:
opts: A price ceiling the stock keeps failing at
A price floor the stock has historically bounced off
quiz.why: Support is a historical floor — where buyers have repeatedly stepped in before.

quiz.q: When a support level breaks, it often becomes:
quiz.why: A broken support commonly flips into resistance, since former buyers may sell there just to break even.

modules.title: The Wyckoff cycle
modules.sub: The four-phase pattern behind almost every DSE run-up — ties into the Syndicate guide

lessons.title: What it is
lessons.body: The Wyckoff cycle describes four repeating phases in a stock's price/volume behaviour: ACCUMULATION (quiet buying at a base, low excitement), MARKUP (a rising phase as the move becomes visible), DISTRIBUTION (topping out, heavy churn as early buyers sell into rising demand), and DECLINE/DUMPING (the fall once distribution completes).
lessons.takeaway: Four phases, roughly in order: quiet Accumulation -> visible Markup -> topping Distribution -> Decline/Dumping.

lessons.title: How it works
lessons.body: Each phase has a different volume/price signature. Accumulation is quiet — price moves little on modest volume. Markup shows rising price WITH rising participation. Distribution shows heavy volume but price struggling to make new highs — a sign supply is meeting demand at the top. Decline shows falling price, often accelerating once support gives way. This app's cycle card names the currently-measured phase for every stock, plus how many weeks it's spent there versus that stock's own typical duration.
lessons.takeaway: Watch how volume behaves at each phase, not just price — that's what separates the phases from each other.

lessons.title: Worked example
lessons.body: This app's own research (see the Syndicate guide) measured that DSE names in Accumulation historically drifted about +1%/6wk (a patient, low-drama zone), Markup roughly flat, and Distribution preceded roughly -0.5%/6wk and falling — i.e. the SAME four-phase pattern that concentrated operators lean on. Recognising which phase a stock is in is exactly how the Syndicate guide's core rule works: buy the quiet despair (Accumulation), never chase the loud Markup, and treat heavy churn near a top (Distribution) as an exit-watch.
lessons.takeaway: This is the direct technical backbone of the Syndicate guide — read that next to see how the phases play out in practice.

quiz.q: Which phase is typically the quietest, lowest-drama one?
quiz.why: Accumulation is the quiet base-building phase — low excitement, modest volume.

quiz.q: Heavy volume with price struggling to make new highs is most associated with:
quiz.why: That volume/price mismatch at a top is the classic Distribution signature.
